chunks.title: Relational model basics
chunks.text: The relational model represents data as tables (relations) of rows (tuples) and columns (attributes). Each row in a table has a unique primary key. Tables are linked by foreign keys — a column in one table that references the primary key of another. The model was proposed by Edgar F. Codd in 1970 and is the foundation of SQL databases (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server). The relational model enforces structure but is less flexible than schemaless stores.

chunks.title: SQL fundamentals
chunks.text: SQL (Structured Query Language) has four families: DDL (CREATE TABLE, ALTER, DROP), DML (SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE), DCL (GRANT, REVOKE), TCL (COMMIT, ROLLBACK). SELECT queries combine FROM (source tables), WHERE (row filter), GROUP BY (aggregation), HAVING (filter on groups), and ORDER BY. JOINs combine tables: INNER JOIN keeps only matching rows; LEFT JOIN keeps all left rows; OUTER JOIN keeps all rows from both sides.

chunks.title: Normalization
chunks.text: Normalization is the process of organizing tables to reduce redundancy and avoid update anomalies. First Normal Form (1NF) eliminates repeating groups — every cell holds a single value. 2NF removes partial dependencies on composite primary keys. 3NF removes transitive dependencies (non-key attribute depending on another non-key attribute). BCNF is a stricter version of 3NF. Over-normalizing requires many JOINs at query time; denormalization trades space for read speed.

chunks.title: ACID properties
chunks.text: ACID describes the four guarantees a transactional database provides. Atomicity: a transaction is all-or-nothing. Consistency: a transaction takes the database from one valid state to another. Isolation: concurrent transactions don't interfere with each other (as if they ran serially). Durability: once committed, the changes survive crashes. Isolation levels (READ UNCOMMITTED, READ COMMITTED, REPEATABLE READ, SERIALIZABLE) trade off concurrency vs. correctness.

chunks.title: Indexes
chunks.text: An index is an auxiliary data structure that speeds up reads at the cost of slower writes and more storage. The most common type is a B+ tree, which keeps data sorted and balanced for O(log n) lookups. Hash indexes give O(1) lookup for exact equality but no range queries. A composite index on (a, b) helps queries that filter on a, or on a AND b, but not on b alone. Indexes on low-cardinality columns (booleans) are usually a waste.

chunks.title: Transactions and concurrency
chunks.text: Concurrent transactions can corrupt data through dirty reads, non-repeatable reads, lost updates, and phantom reads. Two-phase locking acquires all locks before releasing any, preventing many anomalies but risking deadlock. Multi-version concurrency control (MVCC) gives readers a consistent snapshot while writers proceed in parallel — PostgreSQL and Oracle use MVCC. Distributed transactions across databases need two-phase commit (2PC) for atomicity.

chunks.title: Joins and query optimization
chunks.text: A query optimizer translates SQL into an execution plan. Join algorithms: nested loop (O(n*m), fast for small tables), hash join (O(n+m), needs memory to build a hash), merge join (O(n+m) on sorted input). The optimizer uses table statistics (row count, value distribution) and indexes to pick the cheapest plan. EXPLAIN reveals the chosen plan — essential for debugging slow queries.

chunks.title: NoSQL and CAP
chunks.text: NoSQL databases relax SQL guarantees for scale or flexibility. Categories: document stores (MongoDB), key-value (Redis, DynamoDB), column-family (Cassandra), graph (Neo4j). CAP theorem says a distributed system can only guarantee 2 of: Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance. CP systems (HBase) reject writes during partitions; AP systems (Cassandra) accept writes everywhere and reconcile later. SQL databases historically chose CP; many NoSQL stores choose AP.

chunks.title: Entity-Relationship diagrams
chunks.text: An ER diagram models a domain as entities (boxes), attributes (ovals), and relationships (diamonds) before schema design. Cardinality (1:1, 1:N, N:M) describes how many of each entity relate to the other. ER diagrams translate to relational tables: entities become tables, 1:N relationships become foreign keys, N:M relationships become join tables. Drawing the ER first catches design issues before SQL is written.

chunks.title: Subqueries and correlated queries
chunks.text: A subquery is a query nested inside another. Non-correlated subqueries run once and return a value or set; correlated subqueries reference the outer query and run per outer row, often much slower. EXISTS / NOT EXISTS work well with correlated subqueries. Many correlated subqueries can be rewritten as JOINs, which the optimizer usually executes more efficiently.

chunks.title: Window functions
chunks.text: Window functions compute over a defined window of rows without collapsing them like GROUP BY does. ROW_NUMBER(), RANK(), DENSE_RANK() number rows within partitions. LAG()/LEAD() reference adjacent rows. SUM()/AVG() OVER (PARTITION BY x ORDER BY y) compute running totals or moving averages. Window functions replace many self-joins and procedural loops with a single SELECT — far faster and clearer.

chunks.title: Views
chunks.text: A view is a saved query that behaves like a virtual table. SELECT against a view runs the underlying query. Views hide complexity, enforce row/column security, and provide a stable interface as the underlying schema evolves. Materialized views store the result physically, traded freshness for read speed — used in dashboards and reports where slight staleness is acceptable.

chunks.title: Stored procedures and triggers
chunks.text: A stored procedure is server-side code called by name with parameters. Triggers fire automatically on INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE, often for auditing or enforcing rules. Both keep logic close to data, avoiding round-trips. Tradeoffs: harder to version control, test, and migrate than application code. Modern stacks tend to push logic into the application layer and reserve procedures/triggers for invariants that must be database-enforced.

chunks.title: Constraints
chunks.text: Constraints enforce data integrity at the schema level. NOT NULL forbids missing values. UNIQUE prevents duplicates in a column or column set. CHECK enforces a predicate (CHECK (price > 0)). PRIMARY KEY combines NOT NULL and UNIQUE. FOREIGN KEY enforces referential integrity, with ON DELETE CASCADE / SET NULL / RESTRICT controlling what happens when the referenced row goes away. Database-enforced constraints catch bugs the application missed.

chunks.title: Composite and surrogate keys
chunks.text: A composite key combines multiple columns to uniquely identify a row (e.g. order_id + product_id in order_items). A surrogate key is an artificial unique identifier (auto-increment integer or UUID) chosen instead of a natural key. Surrogates are stable across changes (a person can rename, change email — their natural identity isn't a great PK). Composite keys are natural for join tables and time-series partitioning.

chunks.title: Functional dependencies
chunks.text: A functional dependency X → Y means: whenever two rows agree on X, they agree on Y. FDs drive normalization: 2NF removes partial dependencies on a composite key; 3NF removes transitive dependencies (X → Y → Z where Y isn't a key). Armstrong's axioms (reflexivity, augmentation, transitivity) let you derive all FDs from a base set. The closure of a set of attributes reveals candidate keys.

chunks.title: 4NF and 5NF
chunks.text: 4NF eliminates multi-valued dependencies — when a row implies multiple independent sets of values (a student takes multiple courses AND speaks multiple languages should split into two tables). 5NF eliminates join dependencies — situations where decomposing into smaller tables and rejoining is the only way to avoid redundancy. Most real schemas stop at BCNF or 3NF; 4NF and beyond are rare in practice.

chunks.title: Lossless decomposition
chunks.text: A decomposition is lossless if joining the smaller tables exactly reconstructs the original. The test: at least one of the decomposed schemas must contain a superkey of the original. Lossy decompositions lose information — rows that didn't exist appear after a join. Normalization must always be lossless; otherwise you've corrupted the data model.

chunks.title: Isolation level anomalies
chunks.text: READ UNCOMMITTED allows dirty reads (seeing uncommitted writes). READ COMMITTED prevents dirty reads but allows non-repeatable reads (same query, different answer mid-transaction). REPEATABLE READ prevents non-repeatable reads but allows phantoms (new rows matching your WHERE appear). SERIALIZABLE prevents all anomalies but kills concurrency. PostgreSQL's REPEATABLE READ actually gives snapshot isolation, slightly stronger than the standard.

chunks.title: Timestamp ordering
chunks.text: Timestamp ordering is an alternative to locking for serializable transactions. Each transaction gets a timestamp at start; the database orders operations by timestamp. A read or write that would violate the order causes the offending transaction to abort and restart. Optimistic — assumes conflicts are rare. Higher abort rate under contention than locking but no deadlocks.

chunks.title: Recovery and write-ahead logging
chunks.text: WAL guarantees durability: before changing data files, write the change to a sequential log and flush it to disk. After a crash, replay the log to redo committed transactions and undo uncommitted ones. Checkpointing periodically writes dirty pages to data files and records the checkpoint position, bounding the replay work after a crash. PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB, SQLite, and most modern DBMSs use WAL.

chunks.title: Distributed databases
chunks.text: A distributed database spreads data across multiple machines for scale, availability, or geographic locality. Two main strategies: replication (every node holds a copy) and sharding (data partitioned across nodes). Replication trades write throughput for read scalability and fault tolerance. Sharding scales writes but joins across shards are expensive. Many systems do both — sharded clusters with each shard replicated.

chunks.title: Sharding strategies
chunks.text: Range sharding splits data by key range (users A-F on shard 1, G-M on shard 2). Hash sharding hashes the key to a shard for even distribution. Directory-based sharding consults a lookup service. Range queries are fast on range sharding but suffer hotspots; hash sharding spreads load but kills range queries. Resharding is painful — design carefully or use consistent hashing to minimize movement.

chunks.title: Replication patterns
chunks.text: Master-slave replication has one writable primary and multiple read-only replicas. Writes go to primary, replicate asynchronously to replicas. Read-heavy apps scale by routing reads to replicas, accepting some staleness. Master-master allows writes on multiple nodes but requires conflict resolution. Quorum-based replication (Dynamo, Cassandra) writes to W of N replicas and reads from R, with R+W > N giving strong consistency.

chunks.title: Eventual consistency
chunks.text: Eventually consistent stores guarantee that, if no new updates are made, all replicas converge to the same value eventually. Reads may see stale data. This relaxation lets the system stay available during partitions and scale writes. Conflict resolution uses vector clocks, last-writer-wins, or application-defined merge functions. DynamoDB, Cassandra, and many DNS systems are eventually consistent.

chunks.title: Document stores
chunks.text: Document stores (MongoDB, Couchbase, Firestore) hold semi-structured documents — usually JSON — with no fixed schema. Each document can have different fields. Documents are queried by field, sometimes with secondary indexes. Strengths: schema flexibility, natural fit for object-graph data. Weaknesses: weak join story, denormalization risks data drift, transactions limited to single documents historically (improving in newer versions).

chunks.title: Graph databases
chunks.text: Graph databases (Neo4j, Amazon Neptune, ArangoDB) store data as nodes connected by edges, both with properties. They use a query language like Cypher or Gremlin optimized for traversal — multi-hop queries that would be expensive joins in SQL run cheaply. Use cases: social networks, knowledge graphs, recommendation engines, fraud detection. Not a fit for analytics or large aggregate queries.

chunks.title: Time-series databases
chunks.text: Time-series databases (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB, Prometheus) optimize for writes-mostly workloads keyed by (timestamp, metric, tags) and queries over time windows. Storage uses column compression and time-based partitioning. Built-in retention policies drop old data. Used for application metrics, IoT telemetry, financial tick data. Modern relational DBs (PostgreSQL with TimescaleDB extension) often suffice for moderate scale.

chunks.title: Columnar storage
chunks.text: Row-oriented storage groups all columns of a row together — fast for transactional OLTP. Columnar storage groups all values of a column together — fast for analytical OLAP queries that scan one or two columns over millions of rows. Compression is better because adjacent values are similar. Used by Parquet, ORC, Redshift, BigQuery, ClickHouse. Modern hybrid databases blur the distinction.

chunks.title: EXPLAIN and query plans
chunks.text: EXPLAIN shows the optimizer's plan without executing it. EXPLAIN ANALYZE runs the query and reports actual times. Look for sequential scans on large tables (missing index?), nested loops with high row counts (should be hash join?), and bad row estimates (stale statistics — run ANALYZE). The plan is the single most useful debugging tool for slow queries.

chunks.title: Indexing trade-offs
chunks.text: Every index speeds reads but slows writes (every INSERT/UPDATE updates each index). Indexes consume disk space — often as much as the table itself for large tables. Cover indexes include all columns the query needs, avoiding a heap lookup. Partial indexes (WHERE clause on the index) shrink size for filtered queries. Audit indexes regularly — many systems carry unused indexes from old queries.

chunks.title: Database transactions in code
chunks.text: BEGIN/COMMIT/ROLLBACK frame a transaction. In application code, wrap in try/except: commit on success, rollback on exception. Auto-commit (each statement is its own transaction) is the default in many drivers — usually wrong for multi-statement work. Connection pools share connections; using BEGIN without releasing properly leaves transactions hanging, holding locks. Always release.

chunks.title: CAP theorem nuances
chunks.text: CAP says a partitioned distributed system must choose Consistency or Availability — not that you choose two of three. When there's no partition, you get all three. The real decisions are: under partition, do you respond with possibly stale data (AP) or refuse (CP)? PACELC extends CAP: even without partitions, you choose between Latency and Consistency. Most production systems lean AP for user-facing flows and CP for billing.

chunks.title: Database normalization vs denormalization
chunks.text: Normalization eliminates redundancy and update anomalies but requires JOINs. Denormalization duplicates data to avoid JOINs, trading consistency risk for read speed. OLTP systems usually normalize; analytical and reporting systems usually denormalize. Modern systems often do both — write normalized in OLTP, ETL to denormalized warehouse for analytics.
